The Mango-Eating Frontend Dev (20): MongoDB Update Operators

After writing a whole bunch of Mongoose, it suddenly hit me that I'd skipped over the Update Operators part of MongoDB. And the thing is, Update Operators are actually pretty important — so here's a make-up chapter on MongoDB's Update Operators.

Update Operators are a set of methods MongoDB gives you to do things like calculations or renaming fields. But before we get into it, I want to point one thing out. From the exercises in earlier chapters, you've probably noticed that Mongoose lets you write some of the syntax that belongs to the MongoDB chapters too. So some MongoDB syntax we'll just use directly inside Mongoose.

OK, back to Update Operators. Say every time a user runs some feature, their age goes up by 1. Take a second to think about how you'd write that bit of Mongoose. Maybe you'd write something like this:

So if you want age to go up by one — 20 becoming 21 — you need MongoDB's Update Operators. According to the official docs, addition is
, and you use it like this:
— increase it by however much. So writing 1 means +1.
This trick is great for counting blog post views, for example. When a user opens a post, you just bump
But here's the thing — if you go digging through the Update Operators docs, you'll notice there's no separate operator for subtraction. So if you want to drop age by 1 every time, you just use
decides whether to increase or decrease based on whether the value you pass in is positive or negative.

In the end, you'll notice Mongoose pretty often gets mixed with MongoDB syntax. So you can basically think of Mongoose as an extended version of MongoDB — it takes the bits MongoDB is missing and beefs them up. That's exactly why the MongoDB syntax comes first: because it really matters.
